title: Changes of state and latent heat — energy that doesn't raise temperature
examTip: E = mL is the key equation. Two types: latent heat of FUSION (melting/freezing) and VAPORISATION (boiling/condensing). Vaporisation needs far more energy — particles must be fully separated.
introTips: Steam at 100°C causes much worse burns than boiling water at 100°C — latent heat explains exactly why.
Sweating cools you down so effectively because of the enormous latent heat of vaporisation of water.
The flat sections on a heating curve are a favourite exam graph question — you need to explain what's happening at each one.
intro: Have you ever noticed that it takes ages to boil a kettle once the water is already at 100°C — even though the temperature isn't rising? That energy isn't disappearing. It's being used to rip water molecules away from each other as liquid turns to steam. This 'hidden' energy — which changes state without changing temperature — is called latent heat. Understanding it explains everything from why burns from steam are so devastating, to how your fridge keeps food cold, to why sweating on a hot day actually works.

keyPoints.heading: Temperature during changes of state — the heating curve
keyPoints.content: When you heat a substance, its temperature usually rises. But something interesting happens when it reaches its melting point or boiling point: the temperature stays constant, even though energy is still being added. This is shown on a heating curve — a graph of temperature against time (or energy added): - Sloping sections: temperature rising, kinetic energy of particles increasing - Flat sections: temperature constant — a change of state is occurring At the flat section: - Energy is being absorbed by the particles - This energy is breaking the intermolecular bonds between particles - Particles gain potential energy (not kinetic energy) - No increase in kinetic energy → no increase in temperature This energy is called latent heat. The word 'latent' means 'hidden' — the energy is there, but you can't see it in the thermometer reading. When the substance cools, the reverse happens: latent heat is released during freezing and condensation — the flat sections appear again at the same temperatures.
writeBullets: During a change of state, temperature stays CONSTANT even though energy is being added
Energy during state change → breaks intermolecular bonds → increases potential energy
No increase in kinetic energy → no rise in temperature
Heating curve: sloping = temperature rising; flat = change of state
Latent heat — 'hidden' energy absorbed or released during state changes
Flat sections appear at melting point AND boiling point

keyTerms.def: Energy absorbed or released during a change of state — does not cause a temperature change
A graph of temperature vs time (or energy) showing flat sections at melting and boiling points

keyPoints.memTip: Latent = hidden. The energy is there — you just can't see it on a thermometer because it's breaking bonds, not heating the particles.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Draw a full heating curve (ice → water → steam) and make students label all 5 sections: solid heating, melting, liquid heating, boiling, gas heating.

keyPoints.heading: Specific latent heat — the equation E = mL
keyPoints.content: Specific latent heat (L) is the energy needed to change the state of 1 kg of a substance without changing its temperature. Equation: E = mL Where: E = energy (J), m = mass (kg), L = specific latent heat (J/kg) Rearrangements: L = E/m   m = E/L Two types: Specific latent heat of fusion (L_f): energy per kg to melt (or freeze) a substance — solid ↔ liquid - Water: L_f = 334,000 J/kg (334 kJ/kg) Specific latent heat of vaporisation (L_v): energy per kg to boil (or condense) a substance — liquid ↔ gas - Water: L_v = 2,260,000 J/kg (2260 kJ/kg) Why is L_v so much bigger than L_f? Melting only weakens bonds — particles still stay close. Vaporisation completely breaks all bonds — particles must be fully separated and moved far apart. This requires a much greater increase in potential energy, so much more energy is needed.
writeBullets: Specific latent heat (L) — energy per kg to change state, unit: J/kg
E = mL (energy = mass × specific latent heat)
Latent heat of fusion — melting/freezing (solid ↔ liquid); water: 334,000 J/kg
Latent heat of vaporisation — boiling/condensing (liquid ↔ gas); water: 2,260,000 J/kg
Vaporisation needs far more energy than fusion — particles must be completely separated
Rearrangements: L = E/m and m = E/L

keyTerms.def: Energy per kg needed to melt (or freeze) a substance at constant temperature. Unit: J/kg
Energy per kg needed to boil (or condense) a substance at constant temperature. Unit: J/kg
Energy = mass × specific latent heat. The equation for calculating energy in a change of state

keyPoints.memTip: Fusion = melting (think of fusing solid particles into liquid). Vaporisation = boiling (vapour = gas). Vaporisation is always the bigger value.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse fusion (melting) with nuclear fusion — clarify immediately. Also note that L for water is given in exams, so students don't need to memorise the numbers.

keyPoints.heading: Calculations using E = mL
keyPoints.content: The equation E = mL is straightforward, but you need to be careful with units and rearrangements. Worked example 1 — melting ice How much energy is needed to melt 2 kg of ice at 0°C? L_f (water) = 334,000 J/kg E = mL = 2 × 334,000 = 668,000 J = 668 kJ Worked example 2 — condensing steam How much energy is released when 0.5 kg of steam condenses to water at 100°C? L_v (water) = 2,260,000 J/kg E = mL = 0.5 × 2,260,000 = 1,130,000 J = 1130 kJ (Released, because condensation is exothermic) Worked example 3 — finding specific latent heat 500 g of a substance absorbs 150,000 J during melting at constant temperature. L = E/m = 150,000 / 0.5 = 300,000 J/kg Combined calculation — heating ice to steam To convert 1 kg of ice at -10°C to steam at 100°C: 1. Heat ice from -10°C to 0°C: Q = mcΔT = 1 × 2100 × 10 = 21,000 J 2. Melt ice at 0°C: Q = mL_f = 1 × 334,000 = 334,000 J 3. Heat water from 0°C to 100°C: Q = mcΔT = 1 × 4200 × 100 = 420,000 J 4. Boil water at 100°C: Q = mL_v = 1 × 2,260,000 = 2,260,000 J Total = 3,035,000 J ≈ 3 MJ
writeBullets: E = mL — substitute mass in kg and L in J/kg to get energy in J
To condense 0.5 kg steam: E = 0.5 × 2,260,000 = 1,130,000 J (released)
Rearrange for L: L = E/m (remember to convert mass to kg)
Combined problems: add up Q = mcΔT for temperature changes AND E = mL for state changes
State whether energy is absorbed (melting/boiling) or released (freezing/condensing)

keyTerms.def: Specific heat capacity of water = 4200 J/kg°C — used alongside latent heat in combined heating calculations
Energy is released to surroundings — freezing and condensation release latent heat
Energy is absorbed from surroundings — melting and boiling absorb latent heat

keyPoints.memTip: E = mL for state changes. Q = mcΔT for temperature changes. Heating from ice to steam uses BOTH — four separate steps.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Remind students that 500 g must be converted to 0.5 kg before substituting. Unit errors are the main trap in E = mL calculations.

keyPoints.heading: Applications of latent heat
keyPoints.content: Latent heat is not just theory — it has important, sometimes life-saving, practical applications. Sweating and cooling: The specific latent heat of vaporisation of sweat (mainly water) is very high — 2260 kJ/kg. When sweat evaporates from your skin, it carries away an enormous amount of energy from your body. This is why sweating is such an effective cooling mechanism, even in very hot weather. Steam burns: Steam at 100°C causes far worse burns than boiling water at 100°C. The water and steam are at the same temperature — so why? When steam condenses on skin, it releases its latent heat of vaporisation (2260 kJ/kg) directly onto the skin, in addition to the heat from cooling from 100°C. This extra 2260 kJ/kg makes steam burns much more serious. Refrigerators: A working fluid (refrigerant) is pumped around a circuit. Inside the fridge it evaporates (absorbs latent heat from the contents — cooling them). Outside the fridge it condenses (releases that latent heat to the surroundings — this is why the back of a fridge is warm). The refrigerator is effectively pumping heat from inside to outside. Freeze-drying food: Food is frozen then placed in a low-pressure environment so ice sublimates directly to vapour — preserving the food without heat damage.
writeBullets: Sweating — high L_v of water means evaporation carries away huge amounts of energy from body
Steam burns — condensing steam releases 2260 kJ/kg latent heat onto skin, in addition to cooling heat
Steam at 100°C causes worse burns than water at 100°C for the same reason
Refrigerator — refrigerant evaporates inside (absorbs latent heat, cools contents), condenses outside (releases heat)
Fridge back is warm because latent heat is released there during condensation
Freeze-drying — ice sublimes to vapour under low pressure, preserving food without high temperatures

keyTerms.def: The working fluid in a refrigerator that evaporates and condenses as it cycles, transferring heat from inside to outside
Removing water from food by sublimation under low pressure — preserves food without cooking it

keyPoints.memTip: Steam burns = DOUBLE the energy: you get L_v as it condenses, THEN Q = mcΔT as it cools. Always worse than the same mass of boiling water.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The refrigerator explanation is a classic 6-mark question. Practise writing it in full — evaporates inside absorbing latent heat, condenses outside releasing it.

tables.title: Summary of latent heat types
rows: Bonds weaken; particles free to move past each other
Bonds reform; particles lock into lattice
Bonds completely broken; particles far apart
Bonds reform; particles close together again

1.question: How much energy is needed to melt 3 kg of ice at 0°C? (L_f of water = 334,000 J/kg)
1.correctFeedback: Correct! E = mL = 3 × 334,000 = 1,002,000 J = 1002 kJ. The temperature stays at 0°C throughout — all this energy breaks bonds, none raises temperature.
1.wrongFeedback: Use E = mL: multiply the mass (3 kg) by the specific latent heat of fusion (334,000 J/kg). That gives 3 × 334,000 = 1,002,000 J.

3.question: Why does steam at 100°C cause worse burns than boiling water at 100°C, even though they are at the same temperature?
options: When steam condenses on the skin it releases its latent heat of vaporisation (2260 kJ/kg) in addition to the energy from cooling
Steam is faster-moving so it has more kinetic energy than water at the same temperature
Steam is at a higher temperature than water even though the thermometer shows 100°C
Steam contains more particles per unit volume than liquid water
3.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Steam condensing on skin releases an extra 2260 kJ/kg of latent heat before it even begins to cool — this additional energy is what makes steam so much more dangerous.
3.wrongFeedback: Both steam and boiling water are at 100°C — the kinetic energy of the particles is the same. The key difference is latent heat: when steam condenses, it releases 2260 kJ/kg onto the skin before cooling. Water has no latent heat to release — it just cools.

commonMistakes: Thinking temperature rises during a change of state — it stays constant while energy breaks bonds (potential energy increases, kinetic energy does not).
Confusing latent heat of fusion with nuclear fusion — 'fusion' here means melting/freezing (solid ↔ liquid).
Using mass in grams instead of kg in E = mL — always convert to kg first (divide grams by 1000).
Forgetting that condensation and freezing RELEASE latent heat — energy goes from substance to surroundings.
In combined calculations, forgetting to include both the latent heat steps AND the temperature-change steps (E = mL and Q = mcΔT are both needed).
examTips: When asked about a heating curve's flat section, always say: temperature is constant because energy is breaking intermolecular bonds (increasing potential energy), not increasing kinetic energy.
In E = mL calculations, always convert mass to kg and check you are using the correct L (fusion for melting/freezing, vaporisation for boiling/condensing).
For application questions (sweating, steam burns, fridges), explain which change of state is happening, whether energy is absorbed or released, and what effect this has.
revisionCardBullets: Latent heat — energy absorbed or released during a change of state, no temperature change
Temperature stays CONSTANT during a change of state
Energy during state change → breaks bonds → increases potential energy (not kinetic)
E = mL (energy = mass × specific latent heat). Unit of L: J/kg
Latent heat of fusion — melting/freezing (solid ↔ liquid); water: 334,000 J/kg
Latent heat of vaporisation — boiling/condensing (liquid ↔ gas); water: 2,260,000 J/kg
L_v >> L_f because vaporisation fully separates all particles (much more potential energy)
Sweating cools body — high L_v means evaporation removes large amount of energy
Steam burns worse than boiling water — condensing releases 2260 kJ/kg latent heat onto skin
Refrigerator — refrigerant evaporates inside (absorbs heat), condenses outside (releases heat)
Heating curve: slopes = temperature change (Q = mcΔT); flats = state change (E = mL)
Combined calculation: add up all Q = mcΔT and E = mL steps separately
